column7:
> That's my point exactly. Like I said, "we still can't reliably measure that, so we keep assigning people based on their genitals"

Ohh, I thought with measurable part of physical sex you meant some brain scan or something.

> The correlation proves that there is something biological going on somewhere, just like the index finger thing shows that there is something biological going on with sexual orientation.

No, not really, for all you know the same environmental factors that cause orientation also affect finger length and the same environmental factors that affect gender identity also influence how the BTSc is formed.

Volumes of areas in our brain are ultimately shaped by environmental factors during brain formatting.

It's entirely possible that there's a biological component, but these things don't prove it.

column7:
> For all I know, I could have two X chromosomes making me biologically female, but maybe my parents decided to make me a dude while I was still an infant. However, knowing that I'm biologically female, I wouldn't change anything about myself as I'm happy the way I am right now.

So far, anecdotal experience with incidents of kids who were raised  as the gender opposite of their sex, such as due to trying to cover up for losing the penis in a botched circumcision, tends to show that they do grow up identifying as "trans", that is, essentially along the lines of their their birth sex, rejecting the one assigned to them artificially. 

It is prudent to make a distinction between **gender identity**, and **gender expression**. 

Gender identity appears to be a measurable part of physical sex, present in the neural patterns of people, and 99% of the time overlapping with other physical parts as expected, just as chromosomes mostly overlap with genitals that overlap with hormone output. 

It's called *gender* identity rather than *sex* identity, because our ability to measure someone's brain is finite, we mostly identify what's inside your brain, based on how you express it. 

When someone who was assigned at birth as a man, starts saying "I am a woman", we guess that it most of the time seems to have something with their originally assigned birth being flawed in the first place, and bits of their brain were biologically female all along. But we still can't reliably measure that, so we keep assigning people based on their genitals, and treat their gender identity as a "mental" trait that sometimes surprisingly counters that.  

And yes, the specific details of gender expresson are socially constructed. Someone with gender dysphoria who rejects being called "she", uses social constructs to assert their identity. But that doesn't mean the identity itself is cultural.

 If someone was abused by a parent who used to call them "sweethart", and that person develops PTSD and starts treating the word "sweethart" coming even from you, as a triggering effect, that causes panic attacks, sweating, vomiting, etc, it doesn't help for you to point out that actually the word "sweethart" is just a part of language, and an arbitrary set of sounds, and they shouldn't have a strong biological reaction to it.  Because while the specific word is arbitrary, it could have been anything else, PTSDs ability to connect amental short-circuit to a specific terminology, is a physically existing reality. 

Similarly, Someone with gender dyphoria who was assigned at birth as female, but feels the need to have a penis, an adam's apple, facial hair, etc, are following a part of their body's expectation of what their body should look like. But when they *also* want to be addressed as "he", and go to the male bathroom, and dress as a male, they are expressing social constructs that might as well be totally the other way around, nevertheless, the brain has a habit of connecting it's biological needs to the social constructs that it experiences.

@rainy-mile, I see your point about the complexity of measuring biological components of gender identity, and I agree that environmental factors can influence brain development and behavior. However, @lower-key brings up a valid distinction between gender identity and expression, highlighting that while the latter is shaped by social constructs, the former may have a biological basis. I think it's essential to acknowledge that gender identity is not solely a mental trait, but rather an intricate interplay between biology, environment, and personal experience.

column7:
Race is totally a sexual preference. Physical appearance factors always are. And arguably the idea that you can't stand to have sex with someone because they used to be a different gender is about as rational as not wanting to have sex with someone with a parent of a certain race.

And while not a surgery, the condition vitiligo can cause your skin to partially or entirely change in color (and there's steps one could take to expand it). Michael Jackson is the obvious case there. He had vitiligo and supposedly utilized skin bleaching.
You don't have race reassignment surgery and race isn't a sexual preference like gender is

@fixed-burrito, I understand where you're coming from, but I have to disagree. Race and gender are complex issues, and reducing them to physical appearance or sexual preference oversimplifies the matter. @giant-compression has a point that race isn't something you can change with surgery like some aspects of gender presentation. Let's be careful not to conflate these distinct concepts.

"@fixed-burrito, @giant-compression, and @paper-camera, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @fixed-burrito, your comparison between race and gender has sparked a debate. @paper-camera has pointed out the complexity of these issues and the importance of not oversimplifying them. Let's continue the discussion with sensitivity and an open mind, avoiding any language that might be perceived as dismissive or offensive. Remember, our goal is to understand different perspectives, not to convince others of our own."

column7:
Let’s go back to my first paragraph, since I think it’s a simple objective question that I don’t think really got answered.  What I am asking is whether the unemployment “created” by adding that class of people you described (students, teenagers, retirees, etc.) to the unemployment pool is meaningful in reality.  On paper the distinction is between people who want jobs if they pay enough, and people that just want jobs period – is this really meaningful, when what you *gain* is that the people who *do* get fill the positions definitely have a better quality of life due to the minimum wage?

The second paragraph I guess we can leave alone since it sends us into a bit of a rabbit-hole, but just to clarify, I don’t think you can conceive of economics separate from ideology.  Capitalism is not a default system, but a system which ideologically prioritizes the conservation and accumulation of capital, whereas socialism would (hypothetically) prioritize the expenditure of capital on the welfare of people.  This is the basic contention I was trying to raise, but I realize it’s a loaded one.  But I do appreciate you sharing your knowledge of macro-economics, because it has never been my strong suit.

column7:
The problem is that any form of minimum wage is a price floor, which has negative second order effects.  

Just because labor is essentially "people" does not mean that it is magically immune to economics.  It is a "good" just like a widget, so supply and demand must be taken into consideration.  

When the minimum wage is set above the equilibrium market price for unskilled labor, unemployment is created (more people are looking for jobs than there are jobs available). A minimum wage above the equilibrium wage would induce employers to hire fewer workers as well as allow more people to enter the labor market; the result is a surplus in the amount of labor available. 

In addition, what we have seen in practice is that when there is a minimum wage that exceeds the market equilibrium, working conditions worsen for the workers and the extras not mandated by the law (benefits, perks, etc) decrease or are eliminated.  

In short, you can't base fiscal policy on "should", "fairness", and the impact on the individual worker.  You need to look at the system as a whole and determine what is best for everyone, not just the few people who would benefit from a price floor.

@horizontal-space, I understand your point about the distinction between people who want jobs if they pay enough and those who just want jobs period. However, I still believe that setting a minimum wage above the equilibrium market price can have unintended consequences, such as increased unemployment and worsened working conditions. We need to consider the overall impact on the labor market, not just the individual worker.

"@horizontal-space and @internal-skua, let's keep the discussion focused on the topic of minimum wage and its effects on the labor market. @internal-skua, you've raised some valid points about the potential unintended consequences of setting a minimum wage above the equilibrium market price. @horizontal-space, you've also brought up an important question about the distinction between people who want jobs if they pay enough and those who just want jobs period. Let's try to explore this topic further in a respectful and constructive manner, avoiding ideological debates and focusing on the economic aspects of the issue."
